did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) to synthesize dermatology images for four skin lesion classes (melanoma, benign keratosis, actinic keratosis, and basal cell carcinoma) and employs a customized Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify these skin lesions, comparing its performance against pre-trained models like VGG16 and ResNet50.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a novel methodology called 'DermaGAN' using Generative Adversarial Networks to synthesize dermatology images and a customized Convolutional Neural Network for classification. It includes specific sections on methodology, experiments, results, and performance comparisons against pre-trained models (VGG16, ResNet50, etc.), which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: DermaGAN: Enhancing Skin Lesion Classification with Generative Adversarial Networks
journal: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Computing and Communication (ETCC)
brief_summary: This study introduces DermaGAN, a methodology utilizing Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) to synthesize dermatology images for four skin lesion classes: melanoma, benign keratosis, actinic keratosis, and basal cell carcinoma. The synthesized data addresses data scarcity by augmenting the ISIC 2019 and HAM10000 datasets, which were combined with online sources to create a comprehensive training set. A customized Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) was developed and trained on this augmented dataset to classify skin lesions. The main evaluation approach involved a rigorous comparative analysis of the customized CNN against pre-trained models, including VGG16, ResNet50, VGG19, MobileNet, and MobileNetV2. The key result indicates that the customized CNN architecture, trained on GAN-generated images, outperformed the pre-trained models in terms of accuracy and loss measures. The findings highlight the critical role of synthetic image augmentation in enhancing CNN diagnostic skills, particularly in scenarios with sparse annotated data.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four essential classes synthesized by DermaGAN and used for classification in the customized CNN. Included in the combined ISIC 2019 and HAM10000 datasets.

One of the four essential classes synthesized by DermaGAN and used for classification. In HAM10000, referred to as 'benign keratosis-like lesions (bkl)'. Grouped as 'benign' in the final CNN output layer alongside actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, and melanoma.

One of the four essential classes synthesized by DermaGAN and used for classification. In HAM10000, referred to as 'actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen's disease (akiec)'. Grouped as 'actinic' in the final CNN output layer.

all_task_types.target: Four discrete skin lesion classes: melanoma, benign keratosis, actinic keratosis, and basal cell carcinoma.
all_task_types.details: The paper employs a customized Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify skin lesions into four specific diagnostic categories. The model uses a SoftMax activation function in the final layer to generate class probability distributions for these discrete classes. The task is explicitly defined as 'skin lesion classification' and 'categorization of skin lesion classifications'.

all_task_types.target: Synthetic dermatology images for the four skin lesion classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper utilizes a Generative Adversarial Network (DermaGAN) to synthesize realistic dermatoscopic images to augment the training dataset. This is a secondary task used to support the primary classification objective.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 10,015 high-resolution dermatoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions from numerous sources. It includes classes such as actinic keratoses/intraepithelial carcinoma (akiec), basal cell carcinoma (bcc), benign keratosis-like lesions (bkl), dermatofibroma (df), melanoma (mel), melanocytic nevi (nv), and vascular lesions (vas).

use.training: Combined with ISIC 2019 and online sources to form the base dataset for GAN training and subsequent CNN classification.
use.other: Used as a primary source of real images for dataset combination.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 (International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2019)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing over 25,000 dermatoscopic images from eight diagnostic categories, including benign skin lesions, basal cell carcinoma, and melanoma.

use.training: Combined with HAM10000 and online sources to form the base dataset for GAN training and subsequent CNN classification.
use.other: Used as a primary source of real images for dataset combination.

datasets_used.name: Online Dermatological Images
datasets_used.description: Images collected from trusted web sources to diversify the collection and cover a wide range of skin disorders.

use.training: Added to the combined dataset to increase diversity and volume.

datasets_used.name: Synthetic Dataset (GAN-generated)
datasets_used.description: A synthetic dataset created by the DermaGAN model to generate realistic dermatoscopic images for the four target classes.

use.training: Blended with real images to create an augmented training dataset for the customized CNN.
use.other: Generated to address data scarcity and improve model robustness.

datasets_used.name: Custom CNN Training/Test Split
datasets_used.description: The final augmented dataset (real + synthetic) was divided into training and testing sets. Specifically, 100 images per class were prepared for training and testing sets after augmentation.

use.training: Used to train the customized CNN model.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the customized CNN model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on accuracy and loss metrics without statistical significance testing.

Custom CNN (Proposed Model).dataset: Combined ISIC 2019, HAM10000, and Online Sources (Augmented with GAN-generated images)
Custom CNN (Proposed Model).notes: The customized CNN outperformed pre-trained models. It used a dropout rate of 0.2, which was identified as the optimal rate to reduce overfitting without losing information. Dropout rates exceeding 0.2 significantly decreased accuracy, while rates below 0.2 permitted overfitting.

MobileNet.dataset: Combined ISIC 2019, HAM10000, and Online Sources (Augmented with GAN-generated images)

MobileNetV2.dataset: Combined ISIC 2019, HAM10000, and Online Sources (Augmented with GAN-generated images)

ResNet50.dataset: Combined ISIC 2019, HAM10000, and Online Sources (Augmented with GAN-generated images)

VGG16.dataset: Combined ISIC 2019, HAM10000, and Online Sources (Augmented with GAN-generated images)

VGG19.dataset: Combined ISIC 2019, HAM10000, and Online Sources (Augmented with GAN-generated images)

architecture_used.main_model: Customized Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for classification; DermaGAN (Generative Adversarial Network) for image synthesis.
architecture_used.pretraining: The customized CNN was trained from scratch on the augmented dataset (real + GAN-generated images). Pre-trained models (VGG16, ResNet50, VGG19, MobileNet, MobileNetV2) were used for comparative analysis but are not the primary proposed architecture.

discriminator.layers: 4 convolutional layers followed by flattening and dense layers

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly named for CNN, but GAN uses adversarial loss; CNN evaluation used Accuracy and Loss metrics.

preprocessing_techniques: Manual quality control: Repetitious and totally fuzzy photographs were reviewed and eliminated by hand.
Data augmentation: Rotation, flipping, scaling, and color modifications were applied to the real images.
Image resizing: Input images for the CNN were resized to 64x64x3 (RGB).
Dataset balancing: The augmented dataset was balanced to improve performance on underrepresented classes.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to real images during preprocessing: rotation, flipping, scaling, and color modifications.

Used DermaGAN (a customized GAN) to synthesize realistic dermatoscopic images for four classes (melanoma, benign keratosis, actinic keratosis, and basal cell carcinoma). The generator used a 512x1x1 latent vector to produce 64x64x3 images. These synthetic images were blended with real images to augment the training dataset.

The augmented dataset (real + synthetic) was balanced to improve performance on underrepresented classes. Specifically, 100 images per class were prepared for training and testing sets after augmentation.
Repetitious and totally fuzzy photographs were reviewed and eliminated by hand during preprocessing.

conclusions: The proposed DermaGAN methodology, which synthesizes dermatology images for melanoma, benign keratosis, actinic keratosis, and basal cell carcinoma, effectively addresses data scarcity in skin lesion classification.

The customized Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) trained on GAN-generated synthetic images achieved a classification accuracy of 72.00% and a loss of 0.714, outperforming pre-trained models including MobileNet (68.50%), MobileNetV2 (68.25%), VGG16 (66.75%), VGG19 (65.49%), and ResNet50 (47.99%).
Synthetic image augmentation plays a critical role in strengthening CNN diagnostic skills, particularly in scenarios where annotated data is sparse.
A dropout rate of 0.2 was identified as optimal for the customized CNN, as rates exceeding 0.2 significantly decreased accuracy by losing information, while rates below 0.2 permitted overfitting.

The study highlights the potential of deep learning and GAN-based data augmentation to enable earlier and more precise detection of skin cancer, thereby facilitating timely interventions and improving patient outcomes.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small final evaluation dataset, using only 100 images per class for both training and testing (400 total per split), which raises concerns about the statistical robustness and generalizability of the reported 72% accuracy.

The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance comparisons between the customized CNN and pre-trained models (VGG16, ResNet50, etc.), making it difficult to assess if the observed improvements are statistically significant.

The dataset construction involves combining HAM10000, ISIC 2019, and 'trusted web sources' without specifying the exact number of images from online sources or providing detailed provenance, which may introduce selection bias and reproducibility issues.

The customized CNN was trained from scratch on a highly augmented and synthetic-enriched dataset, whereas the baseline models (VGG16, ResNet50, etc.) were pre-trained models; this creates an unfair comparison where the proposed model benefits from extensive custom augmentation while baselines do not.

The input image resolution is low (64x64 pixels), which may limit the model's ability to capture fine-grained dermatological features necessary for accurate skin lesion classification compared to studies using higher resolutions.

The paper claims to address data scarcity by synthesizing images for only four classes (melanoma, benign keratosis, actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma), ignoring other classes present in the source datasets (e.g., dermatofibroma, melanocytic nevi, vascular lesions), which limits the clinical scope of the solution.

There is no mention of external validation on a completely independent dataset; the model is evaluated only on the split of the combined augmented dataset, limiting the assessment of its generalizability to new clinical settings.

The GAN generator uses a latent vector of size 512x1x1 and produces 64x64x3 images; the complexity of the GAN architecture relative to the small dataset size may lead to overfitting or mode collapse, although this is not explicitly analyzed in the results.
